chapters.title: paper

overview.summary: This is the complete Maharashtra Board Std. X Geography question paper from July 2023, reproduced with the full official marking scheme and model solutions. It covers Q.1 (MCQs), Q.2 (matching), Q.3 (one-sentence answers), Q.4 (map work on India/Brazil), Q.5 (geographical reasons), Q.6 (graph work), and Q.7 (detailed answers), spanning topics from India-Brazil comparisons, wildlife, population density, tourism, and historical background.
youWillLearn: The exact format and time/marks structure of the Std. X Geography board paper
Key comparative facts about India and Brazil (government, area, exports, population)
How population density and tourism statistics are read from maps and graphs
Geographical reasoning for coastal ports, rainfall patterns, and cyclones
The historical background of India and Brazil since independence
How to answer field-visit and wildlife-diversity questions in detail

notes.content: Exam Format & General Instructions

notes.title: Time and Marks
notes.content: Time : 2 Hours] [Max. Marks : 40
rules: Figures to the right indicate full marks.
For Q. No. 4 (A) use supplied outline map of India/Brazil and tie it to the main answer-book.
For Q. No 6 (A) use the graph paper supplied to you and attach that supplement to the main answer-book.
Draw neat diagrams and sketches wherever necessary while writing answers.
Use of stencil is allowed for drawing maps wherever necessary while writing answers.
Answers should be written in black or blue pen only. Answers written in pencil will not be considered.
Use of pencil or colour pencil is allowed for diagrams, graphs and map work.

notes.content: Q. 5 — Give Geographical Reasons (Any Two) (6 Marks)

notes.title: (1) Fewer natural ports on India's eastern coast (Ch. 3)
notes.content:
There are fewer natural ports on the eastern coast of India.

(1) The eastern coast of India is formed due to the depositional work of eastward flowing rivers meeting the Bay of Bengal.
(2) Most of the western coast of India is a coast of submergence. On the other hand eastern coast of India is a coast of emergence. As its effect, sea is deeper in the west than sea on the eastern coast.
(3) Westward flowing rivers meeting the Arabian Sea form estuaries at many places on the western coast. On the other hand, eastward flowing rivers meeting the Bay of Bengal form deltas at many places on the eastern coast. As its effect, it becomes difficult for ships to reach the east coast covered with sediments. Therefore, there are fewer natural ports on the eastern coast of India.

notes.title: (2) Low rainfall in NE Brazilian Highlands (Ch. 4)
notes.content:
The northeastern part of Brazilian Highlands receives very less rainfall.

(1) The winds blowing from the Southern Atlantic Ocean in the southeast and the northeast direction are obstructed by the Brazilian Highlands.
(2) These winds move upward along with the slopes of the Brazilian Highlands. At higher altitude, the condensation process takes place and windward side of Brazilian Highlands receives more rain.
(3) When these winds cross the top of Brazilian Highlands, go to other side of mountain, these winds are dry. As its effect, the leeward side of the Brazilian Highlands gets less rainfall. Therefore, the northeastern part of Brazilian Highlands receives very less rainfall.

notes.title: (3) Rare tropical cyclones in Brazil (Ch. 4)
notes.content:
Tropical cyclones occur rarely in Brazil.

(1) In the northern coastal region near equator, the differences in the temperatures are negligible.
(2) In this region, the winds move vertically.
(3) The convergence zone of the trade winds is also found to be weak in this region. Therefore, tropical cyclones occur rarely in Brazil.

notes.title: (4) Dense railway network in North Indian Plains (Ch. 9)
notes.content:
A dense network of railways has developed in North Indian plains.

(1) The North Indian Plains are low lying. There are not many hills in this region. The density of population in this region is quite high.
(2) The agriculture, trade, commerce, industries are well developed in the North Indian Plains.
(3) The need for transport of goods as well as need of passenger transport is very high in this region. Therefore, a dense network of railways has developed in the North Indian Plains.

notes.content: Q. 7 — Answer in Detail (Any Two) (8 Marks)

notes.title: (1) Historical background of India and Brazil (Ch. 2)
notes.content:
(A) Historical background of India :
(1) For nearly, one and a half century, India was under British rule.
(2) India got independence on 15th August, 1947.
(3) Since independence, India has followed federal parliamentary republic type of government.
(4) Within twenty years after independence, India faced and overcame problems like three wars, famines in various parts and many other financial issues.
(5) Despite these issues, today India is a major developing country of the world.
(6) Today, India is considered as one of the major global markets.
(7) After independence, various economic reforms took place in India. These reforms boosted the rate of economic development of India.
(8) The proportion of working people (youth) in India's population is comparatively high. Therefore, India is looked upon as a young country.

(B) Historical background of Brazil :
(1) Brazil was under the rule of Portuguese for more than three centuries. It got independence on 7th September 1922.
(2) From 1930 to 1985, Brazil had a military government. From 1985, it has federal presidential republic type of government.
(3) In the late 20th century, Brazil has faced many financial difficulties and has overcame them successfully.
(4) Today, Brazil is seen as one of the important countries contributing to economic growth of the world and a major global market in future.

notes.title: (2) Managing litter during a field visit (Ch. 1)
notes.content:
We will manage the litter during the field visit in the following ways :
(1) Carrying containers/bags/dustbins to collect litter dumped on a field.
(2) Informing everyone to not to dump trash/waste on a field during field visit.
(3) Creating awareness about cleanliness using charts and banners or performing street play, etc.
(4) Informing and requesting officials of local corporations for taking immediate remedial actions against litter/waste dump on a field.

notes.title: (3) Diversity of wildlife in Brazil (Ch. 5)
notes.content:
The diversity of wildlife in Brazil is explained with the help of the following points :
(1) Brazil is blessed with the rich wildlife. It has the highest diversity in the wildlife in the world.
(2) Huge anacondas are mainly found in the swampy areas of Pantanal. The animals like guinea pigs, crocodiles, alligators, monkeys, pumas, leopards, etc. are commonly seen in Brazil.
(3) Variety of fish is found in Brazil. Swordfish are mainly found in Atlantic Sea near Brazil. Pink dolphins and piranhas are mainly seen in rivers in Brazil.
(4) Huge sized condors that flow high in the sky are seen in Brazil. Various types of parrots, macaws, flamingoes are the other major birds as well as millions of varieties of insects are also found in Brazil.

chapters.title: Question Set 1: Choose the Correct Options

overview.summary: This Question Set drills the MCQ portion (Q.1) of the board paper, organised chapter-by-chapter across all 9 Geography chapters (Field Visit through Tourism, Transport & Communication). Each chapter offers several fill-in-the-blank style MCQs with the official answer key.
youWillLearn: Key facts and terminology from each of the 9 Geography chapters in MCQ form
Common board-exam MCQ phrasing and distractor patterns
Quick recall of comparative India-Brazil facts across chapters
Correct terminology for field visit tools, physiography, climate, vegetation, population, settlements, economy, and transport

notes.content: Question Set 1: Choose the Correct Options (Q.1, 4 Marks)

notes.title: Instructions
notes.content:
Q. Write the following statements by choosing the correct options : (4 Statements; 1 mark each)

Remember : (1) Four statements will be given in the question paper. (2) Choose the correct option, rewrite the statement and underline the option choosen. (3) Do not overwrite. (4) Do not change the sequence of statements given in the question paper.
Chapter 1: Field Visit

notes.title: Chapter 1: Field Visit — Questions & Answers
notes.content:
(1) ___ is used for knowing directions.
(a) Scale  (b) Notebook  (c) Compass  (d) Camera

(2) ___ is not an essential material of field visit.
(a) Notebook  (b) Map  (c) Bar chart  (d) Camera

(3) We should carry ___ for a field visit to collect information.
(a) chair  (b) blackboard  (c) water bottle  (d) questionnaire

Ans. (1) Compass  (2) Bar chart  (3) questionnaire.
Chapter 2: Location and Extent

notes.title: Chapter 2: Location and Extent — Questions & Answers
notes.content:
(1) India's southernmost point is known as ___.
(a) Lakshadweep  (b) Kanyakumari  (c) Indira Point  (d) Port Blair

(2) India is located in the ___ hemispheres of the earth.
(a) Northern and Eastern  (b) Southern and Western  (c) Northern and Western  (d) Southern and Eastern

(3) ___ these two countries in South America do not share their border with Brazil.
(a) Chile – Ecuador  (b) Argentina – Bolivia  (c) Columbia – French Guiana  (d) Suriname – Uruguay

(4) Brazil is famous for ___ dance.
(a) Samba  (b) Bihu  (c) Bhangda  (d) Bharatnatyam

(5) Arabian Sea is located to the ___ of India.
(a) east  (b) west  (c) south  (d) north

Ans. (1) Indira Point  (2) Northern and Eastern  (3) Chile – Ecuador  (4) Samba  (5) west.
Chapter 3: Physiography and Drainage

notes.title: Chapter 3: Physiography and Drainage — Questions & Answers
notes.content:
(1) Brazil is covered mainly by ___.
(a) highlands  (b) plains  (c) mountainous region  (d) dissected hills

(2) Like Brazil, India too has ___.
(a) high mountains  (b) ancient plateau  (c) west-flowing rivers  (d) snow-capped mountains

(3) Amazon is a large river in the world. Near its mouth ___.
(a) deltaic regions are found  (b) no deltas are found  (c) deposition of sediments occurs  (d) fishing is done

(4) The total coastline of India is ___ km long.
(a) 7,100  (b) 7,400  (c) 7,700  (d) 7,500

(5) The Lakshadweep Islands of the Arabian Sea are ___.
(a) made from the part separated from the mainland  (b) coral islands  (c) volcanic islands  (d) continental islands

Ans. (1) highlands  (2) ancient plateau  (3) no deltas are found  (4) 7,500  (5) coral islands.
Chapter 4: Climate

notes.title: Chapter 4: Climate — Questions & Answers
notes.content:
(1) India's climate is of ___ type.
(a) humid  (b) monsoon  (c) equatorial  (d) cold

(2) Brazil gets rainfall from south-east and north-east ___ winds.
(a) monsoon  (b) trade (eastern)  (c) anti-trade  (d) cyclonic

(3) In Brazil, ___ receives orographic rainfall.
(a) Guyana Highlands  (b) windward side of the Great Escarpment  (c) Amazon river basin  (d) Paraguay–Parana river basin

(4) As the ___ extent of Brazil is more, there is a variety in climatic conditions.
(a) equatorial  (b) latitudinal and longitudinal  (c) longitudinal  (d) latitudinal

Ans. (1) monsoon  (2) trade (eastern)  (3) windward side of the Great Escarpment  (4) latitudinal and longitudinal.
Chapter 5: Natural Vegetation and Wildlife

notes.title: Chapter 5: Natural Vegetation and Wildlife — Questions & Answers
notes.content:
(1) Teak is mainly found in the ___ type of forest.
(a) coastal  (b) thorny and bush  (c) deciduous  (d) coniferous

(2) The evergreen rainforests in Brazil are called ___.
(a) Mediterranean forest  (b) lungs of the world  (c) coffee pot of the world  (d) roca

(3) In the swampy areas of Pantanal, huge ___ are found.
(a) Piranhas/Condor  (b) Macaws/Pink Dolphins  (c) Anacondas  (d) Snow leopard/Macaws

(4) In the mixed forests at foothills of Himalayas ___ trees are found in plenty.
(a) Sal  (b) Sundar  (c) Khejri  (d) Teak

(5) In India, thorny vegetation is found in the state of ___.
(a) Maharashtra  (b) Goa  (c) Rajasthan  (d) Kerala

(6) ___ forests are seen in the northernmost part of India.
(a) Thorny  (b) Deciduous  (c) Himalayan  (d) Evergreen

(7) ___ trees are found in the coastal forests in India.
(a) Deodar  (b) Pine  (c) Sundari  (d) Sandalwood

Ans. (1) deciduous  (2) lungs of the world  (3) Anacondas  (4) Sal  (5) Rajasthan  (6) Himalayan  (7) Sundari.
Chapter 6: Population

notes.title: Chapter 6: Population — Questions & Answers
notes.content:
(1) Sex ratio means the number of females per ___ males in a region.
(a) hundred  (b) lakh  (c) thousand  (d) million

(2) Populationwise, India is ___ largest country in the world.
(a) first  (b) fifth  (c) seventh  (d) second

(3) Brazil is the ___ populated country in the South American continent.
(a) less  (b) medium  (c) very less  (d) very high

(4) Populationwise, Brazil is ___ largest country in the world.
(a) second  (b) third  (c) fourth  (d) fifth

(5) With reference to area, India stands ___ in the world.
(a) fifth  (b) seventh  (c) first  (d) ninth

(6) On the distribution of population ___ factor affects.
(a) physiographic  (b) sex-ratio  (c) literacy  (d) life expectancy

Ans. (1) thousand  (2) second  (3) very high  (4) fifth  (5) seventh  (6) physiographic.
Chapter 7: Human Settlements

notes.title: Chapter 7: Human Settlements — Questions & Answers
notes.content:
(1) The concentration of settlements is related to ___ major factor.
(a) proximity to the sea  (b) plain region  (c) availability of water  (d) climate

(2) In Narmada valley ___ type of settlements are found.
(a) linear  (b) dispersed  (c) nucleated  (d) star-shaped

(3) In northeastern part of Brazil, ___ type of settlements are found.
(a) nucleated  (b) linear  (c) dispersed  (d) star-shaped

(4) Dispersed settlements are found ___ in India.
(a) near the rivers  (b) near the transport routes  (c) in hilly areas  (d) in industrial regions

(5) Concentrated settlements are found in Narmada Valley because of ___.
(a) forested land  (b) cultivable land  (c) undulating topography  (d) industries

(6) ___ state has the least urbanisation in Brazil.
(a) Para  (b) Amapa  (c) Espirito Santo  (d) Parana

Ans. (1) availability of water  (2) nucleated  (3) dispersed  (4) in hilly areas  (5) cultivable land  (6) Para.
Chapter 8: Economy and Occupations

notes.title: Chapter 8: Economy and Occupations — Questions & Answers
notes.content:
(1) The economies of India and Brazil are of the ___ type.
(a) underdeveloped/undeveloped  (b) developed  (c) developing  (d) highly developed

(2) In India, salt is produced on a large scale in the state of ___.
(a) Gujarat  (b) Goa  (c) Punjab  (d) West Bengal

(3) India's per capita income is less than Brazil due to ___.
(a) low national income  (b) massive population  (c) big family size  (d) low food grain production

(4) The economy of Brazil is mainly dependent on the ___ activities there.
(a) primary  (b) secondary  (c) tertiary  (d) quaternary

(5) Brazil is the largest exporter of ___ in the world.
(a) mineral oil  (b) tea  (c) bajra  (d) coffee

Ans. (1) developing  (2) Gujarat  (3) massive population  (4) tertiary  (5) coffee.
Chapter 9: Tourism, Transport and Communication

notes.title: Chapter 9: Tourism, Transport and Communication — Questions & Answers
notes.content:
(1) In India, the railway network is relatively less dense in the state of ___.
(a) Uttar Pradesh  (b) Maharashtra  (c) Tamil Nadu  (d) Rajasthan

(2) In Brazil ___ a southwest flowing river is used for waterways.
(a) Puraguaco  (b) Parnaiba  (c) Purus  (d) Parana

(3) In Brazil, the telecommunication services are not well expanded in the ___ region.
(a) southern  (b) central  (c) eastern  (d) northwestern

(4) The most common method of transportation in Brazil is ___.
(a) airways  (b) waterways  (c) roadways  (d) railways

(5) ___ have been important in the growth of the Indian economy.
(a) Waterways  (b) Airways  (c) Roadways  (d) Railways

Ans. (1) Rajasthan  (2) Parana  (3) northwestern  (4) roadways  (5) Railways.

notes.content: Question Set 9: Answer in One Sentence (Q.3, 4 Marks)

notes.title: Instructions
notes.content:
Q. Answer the following questions in one sentence each : (4 out of 5 questions; 1 mark each)

Remember : (1) Five questions will be given in question paper. (2) Students are expected to answer any four of them. (3) Remember that this question is objective type. So answer to this question should be strictly in one sentence each only.
Chapter 1: Field Visit

notes.question: What is field visit?
notes.qnaAnswer: Collecting geographical and cultural information by actually paying a visit to a particular place is called 'field visit'.

notes.question: What types of information of a field can be collected by field visit?
notes.qnaAnswer: Geographical, social, economic, historical, cultural, etc. types of information of a field can be collected by field visit.

notes.content: Chapter 2: Location and Extent

notes.question: Areawise, which is the largest state in India?
notes.qnaAnswer: Areawise, Rajasthan is the largest state in India.

notes.question: In which Hemispheres is India situated?
notes.qnaAnswer: India is situated in both the Northern Hemisphere and the Eastern Hemisphere.

notes.question: In which Hemispheres is Brazil situated?
notes.qnaAnswer: Brazil is situated in the Northern, Southern and Western Hemispheres.

notes.question: Which parallels of latitude pass through Brazil?
notes.qnaAnswer: Equator and Tropic of Capricorn pass through Brazil.

notes.question: Brazil is mainly located in which hemisphere?
notes.qnaAnswer: Brazil is mainly located in southern hemisphere.

notes.question: Which is the popular sport of Brazil?
notes.qnaAnswer: Football is the popular sport of Brazil.

notes.question: How many union territories are there in India?
notes.qnaAnswer: There are eight union territories in India.

notes.question: By which name is the southernmost point of India known?
notes.qnaAnswer: The southernmost point of India is known as Indira Point.

notes.content: Chapter 3: Physiography and Drainage

notes.question: Which is the highest peak in Brazil?
notes.qnaAnswer: Pico de Neblina is the highest peak in Brazil.

notes.question: Which major river systems are included in Himalayan drainage?
notes.qnaAnswer: Sindhu river system and Ganga river system are included in Himalayan drainage.

notes.question: From where does Ganga river originate?
notes.qnaAnswer: Ganga river originates at Bhagirathi (at Gangotri) from Alaknanda glacier.

notes.question: By which names is the desert region of Rajasthan known?
notes.qnaAnswer: The desert region of Rajasthan is known as 'Thar desert' or 'Marusthali'.

notes.question: Through which states does river Kaveri flow?
notes.qnaAnswer: River Kaveri flows through the states of Karnataka and Tamil Nadu.

notes.question: From where does Amazon river originate?
notes.qnaAnswer: Amazon river originates from the eastern slopes of Andes Mountains in Peru.

notes.question: How many kilometres of a coastline does India have?
notes.qnaAnswer: India has a coastline of about 7500 kilometres.

notes.question: How many kilometres of a coastline does Brazil have?
notes.qnaAnswer: Brazil has a coastline of about 7400 kilometres.

notes.content: Chapter 4: Climate

notes.question: What is rainshadow region in Brazil called?
notes.qnaAnswer: Rainshadow region in Brazil is called as 'Drought Quadrilateral'.

notes.question: Which type of climate is found to the south of the Tropic of Capricorn in Brazil?
notes.qnaAnswer: Temperate type of climate is found to the south of the Tropic of Capricorn in Brazil.

notes.question: In which part of Brazil does less variations in temperature found?
notes.qnaAnswer: Less variations in temperature is found in the equatorial region at coastal part in Brazil.

notes.question: What is the average rainfall in the Amazon river basin?
notes.qnaAnswer: The average rainfall in the Amazon river basin is about 2000 mm.

notes.question: By which name is the northeast part of Brazil known?
notes.qnaAnswer: The northeast part of Brazil is known as 'Drought Quadrilateral'.

notes.question: Which form of rain is found in Himalayan region?
notes.qnaAnswer: Orographic type of rain is found in Himalayan region.

notes.question: What is sex ratio?
notes.qnaAnswer: Sex ratio is the number of females per 1000 males in population.

notes.question: What is average life expectancy?
notes.qnaAnswer: Average life expectancy is the number of years a person could expect to live since its birth.

notes.question: How many per cent of the world's total land is occupied by India?
notes.qnaAnswer: Nearly 2.41 per cent of the world's total land is occupied by India.

notes.question: How many per cent of the world's total population is in India?
notes.qnaAnswer: Nearly 17.5 per cent of the world's total population is in India.

notes.content: Chapter 7: Human Settlements

notes.question: Which factors lead to variety of settlements?
notes.qnaAnswer: The factors like climate, availability of water, slope of land, fertility of land, level of development, etc. lead to variety of settlements.

notes.question: Which type of settlements are found in the North Indian Plains?
notes.qnaAnswer: Nucleated settlements are found in the North Indian Plains.

notes.question: Which is the most urbanized state in India?
notes.qnaAnswer: Goa is the most urbanized state in India.

notes.question: What type of settlements are found in the central part of India?
notes.qnaAnswer: Dispersed settlements are found in the central part of India.

notes.question: In which river basins in India are the nucleated settlements seen?
notes.qnaAnswer: The nucleated settlements in India are seen in the river basins of Ganga, Narmada, Godavari, Kaveri, etc.

notes.question: Which rivers meet near Manaus in Brazil?
notes.qnaAnswer: Negro river and Amazon river meet near Manaus in Brazil.

notes.question: Which type of settlements are found in the southeastern part of Brazil?
notes.qnaAnswer: Nucleated settlements are found in the southeastern part of Brazil.

notes.content: Chapter 8: Economy and Occupations

notes.question: Which currency is mainly used for international trade?
notes.qnaAnswer: US dollar is the currency mainly used for international trade.

notes.question: What type of agriculture is mainly practised in India?
notes.qnaAnswer: Subsistence agriculture is mainly practised in India.

notes.question: Which type of economy exists in Brazil?
notes.qnaAnswer: Mixed economy exists in Brazil.

notes.question: At which places in India is the fresh water fishing carried out?
notes.qnaAnswer: In India, the fresh water fishing is carried out in rivers, canals, irrigation channels, tanks, ponds, lakes, etc.

notes.question: Which countries are the major trading partners of Brazil?
notes.qnaAnswer: Germany, USA, Canada, Italy, Argentina, Saudi, India, etc. countries are the major trading partners of Brazil.

notes.question: Which countries are the major trading partners of India?
notes.qnaAnswer: UK, USA, Germany, Japan, China, Russia, Brazil, etc. countries are the major trading partners of India.

notes.question: Which type of occupations give a boost to the development of a country's economy?
notes.qnaAnswer: Tertiary type of occupations give a boost to the development of a country's economy.

notes.question: Which longitude in India is considered as Indian Standard Time?
notes.qnaAnswer: 82° 30' E longitude in India is considered as Indian Standard Time.

notes.question: For what purposes do foreign tourists visit India?
notes.qnaAnswer: Foreign tourists visit India for exploring Indian heritage and culture, to carry our adventurous or business activities and to receive health services, etc.

notes.question: Which is the cheapest mode of transport?
notes.qnaAnswer: Waterways is the cheapest mode of transport.

notes.question: Through which city does the standard time meridian of India pass?

notes.title: (3) Western Ghats and Eastern Ghats
rows: Western Ghats is to the west of Deccan Plateau.
Eastern Ghats is to the east of Deccan Plateau.

Eastern Ghats is comparatively wider and its altitude is comparatively low.

notes.title: (4) Lakshadweep Islands Group and Andaman-Nicobar Islands Group

Andaman-Nicobar Islands group is located in the Bay of Bengal.

Islands in Andaman-Nicobar Islands Group are comparatively large in extent and have hilly regions.

notes.title: (5) Forest Types of India and Forest Types of Brazil
rows: The location of India is far away from equator. Therefore, the tropical evergreen forests are not seen in India.
The northern region near equator in Brazil receives abundant sunshine and heavy rainfall. Therefore, the tropical evergreen forests are seen in northern part of Brazil.
Himalayan forests are found in northern part and snow covered mountainous regions in India.
No snow covered mountainous regions are found in Brazil. Therefore, the Himalayan forests that are seen in India are not seen in Brazil.

notes.title: Instructions
notes.content:
Q. Write short notes : (2 out of 3 questions; 2 marks each)

Remember : (1) Three questions will be given in question paper. (2) Students are expected to write answers to any two questions. (3) Two to four points are expected in answer.
Chapter 1: Field Visit

notes.title: (1) Items to be carried for a field visit.
notes.content: For the answer, refer to Q.4(A) from Question Set 20.

notes.title: (2) Precautions to be taken continuously during field visit.
notes.content:
We will take the following precautions continuously during field visit :
(1) We will ensure safety of oneself and others and will carry identity card, first aid box, etc. with oneself for emergency circumstances.
(2) We will strictly obey the instructions given by teachers.
(3) During field visit, we will try to gather information about local circumstances by conversing with the local people.
(4) We will also ensure that one does not harm the environment in any way during field visit.
Chapter 2: Location and Extent

notes.title: (3) The latitudinal and longitudinal extent of India and Brazil.
notes.content:
(A) India : (1) Latitudinal extent : The latitudinal extent of the mainland of India is from 8°4' N latitude to 37°6' N latitude. Indira Point is the southernmost point of India's territory. It is located on Nicobar island at the 6°45' latitude.
(2) Longitudinal extent : The longitudinal extent of India is from 68°7' E to 97°25' E.
(B) Brazil : (1) Latitudinal extent : The latitudinal extent of Brazil is from 5°15' N to 33°45' S.
(2) Longitudinal extent : The longitudinal extent of Brazil is from 34°45' W to 73°48' W.

notes.title: (4) Location and extent of Brazil.
notes.content:
(1) Brazil is located in the northern part of the continent of South America. It is located in the Northern, Southern and Western Hemispheres.
(2) The latitudinal extent of Brazil is 5°15' N to 33°45' S.
(3) The longitudinal extent of Brazil is 34°45' W to 73°48' W.
(4) The Equator passes through the northern part of Brazil. The Tropic of Capricorn passes through the southern part of Brazil.

notes.title: (5) Historical background of Brazil.
notes.content: For the answer, refer to Q. 7 (1) (B) of the Solved Question Paper given in Section 1 of this book.
Chapter 3: Physiography and Drainage

notes.title: (6) Western coastal plain.
notes.content:
(1) The western coastal region of India borders the Arabian Sea.
(2) At places, spurs taking off from the Western Ghats have extended right up to the western coast. As its effect, the western coast is rocky in nature.
(3) The width of the western coast is comparatively less.
(4) Westward flowing rivers meeting the Arabian Sea form estuaries and not deltas on the western coast.

notes.title: (7) The coasts of Brazil.
notes.content:
(1) The coastline of Brazil extends for approximately 7400 km. It is divided into the northern coast and the eastern coast.
(2) The coast extending from Amapa province in the north to Rio Grande do Norte is known as the northern coast/North Atlantic coast. The coast extending from Rio Grande do Norte to the south is known as the eastern coast/South Atlantic coast.
(3) Northern coast is a low-lying region as Amazon and its many tributaries flow to the northern coast and meet North Atlantic Ocean. Marajo island, Marajo and Sao Marcos Bays lie on this coast. Marajo coastal island lying on the northern coast is located between the mouths of River Amazon and River Tocantins.
(4) Many small rivers flow to the eastern coast and meet South Atlantic Ocean. Sao Francisco is the only major river meeting the South Atlantic Ocean in this region. Many beaches and sand dune complexes lie on the eastern coast of Brazil. In some areas, the eastern coast of Brazil is protected by coral reefs and atoll islands.

notes.title: (8) Paraguay – Parana system.
notes.content:
(1) Paraguay and Parana rivers flow from the southwest region of Brazil.
(2) These two rivers flow in southwest direction in Brazil and meet River Plata in neighbouring country of Argentina.
(3) These two rivers collect their headwaters from the southern slopes of Brazilian Highlands.
(4) These two rivers form the plains in the southwest region of Brazil.

notes.title: (9) The characteristics of the Western Ghats.
notes.content:
(1) The Western Ghats is a mountain range that runs north to south parallel to the Western Coast of the Indian peninsula.
(2) It demarcates the western side of Deccan plateau. Many peninsular rivers like Godavari, Krishna, Kaveri have their source in Western Ghats.
(3) The Western Ghats is not a single mountain like a wall. It includes series of ranges. At some places, it has regions of very low altitude too.
(4) The Western Ghats separate the westward flowing rivers meeting to Arabian Sea from the eastward flowing rivers meeting the Bay of Bengal.

notes.title: (10) Himalayas.
notes.content:
(1) Himalayas is one of the young fold mountains in the world. It extends from Pamir Knot in Tajikistan in the west to the east. Himalayas is one of the major mountain systems of the continent of Asia.
(2) In India, Himalayas extends from Jammu and Kashmir in the north to Arunachal Pradesh in the northeast. Himalayas as well as many other parallel mountain ranges get included in Himalayas mountain system. Shivalik is the southernmost mountain range of Himalayas. It is the youngest mountain range of Himalayas.
(3) As we move from Shivalik range in the south to the north, we come across Lesser Himalayas, Greater Himalayas and Trans Himalayan ranges respectively. These ranges are young to old respectively.
(4) The Himalayan mountain ranges can also be divided from the west to the east into Western Himalayas (or Kashmir Himalaya), Central Himalaya (or Kumaun Himalayas) and Eastern Himalaya (Assam Himalayas).

notes.title: (11) The Indian peninsula.
notes.content:
(1) The Indian peninsula lies to the south of North Indian Plains. This region tapers towards the Indian Ocean.
(2) Many plateaus and hill ranges are included in the Indian peninsula. The Aravalis range is located in the north of the Indian peninsula. It is the oldest mountain range in the Indian peninsula.
(3) The Indian peninsula also includes a series of plateaus bordering the Plains. Maharashtra Plateau, Karnataka-Telangana Plateau, Chhota Nagpur Plateau, etc. are the main plateaus in this region.
(4) Vindhyas and Satpuda ranges are located in the central part of the Indian peninsula. It also includes mountainous/hilly regions of the Western Ghats in the west and the Eastern Ghats in the east.

notes.title: (12) Guyana Highlands.
notes.content:
(1) The main region of the Guyana Highlands is in Venezuela. Guyana Highlands extents from the west to French Guiana in the east.
(2) Guyana Highlands is in the northern part of Brazil. It is spread in the states of Roraima, Para and Amapa.
(3) The region of comparatively low height of Guyana Highlands is found in Brazil. The highest peak of Brazil, Pico de Neblina lies on the border between Brazil and Venezuela. The height of this peak is nearly 3014 m.
(4) Guyana Highlands and Brazilian Highlands together form the core of the continent of South America.
Chapter 4: Climate

notes.title: (13) Retreating monsoon.
notes.content:
(1) The southwest monsoon winds flowing from the mainland of India are obstructed by the Himalayan ranges in the north resulting their return journey backward towards the Indian Ocean.
(2) These winds blowing from northeast to southwest direction again brings monsoon to Indian Peninsula. This monsoon is called as retreating monsoon.
Chapter 5: Natural Vegetation and Wildlife

notes.title: (14) The evergreen forests in India.
notes.content:
(1) The evergreen forests in India are found in the regions receiving abundant sunlight and annual average rainfall more than 2000 mm. These forests are found in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, the Western Ghats, the coastline of peninsular India, and the Assam region in the north-east.
(2) The trees like mahogany, rosewood, rubber, sandalwood, etc. are found in the evergreen forests in India. Various types of creepers are also found in these forests.
(3) The trees from evergreen forests have broad and green leaves. Their wood is found to be hard, heavy and durable.
(4) Many species of vegetation and wildlife is found in evergreen forests in India.

notes.title: (15) The coastal type of vegetation in India.
notes.content:
(1) In India, the coastal type of vegetation is found along the coast, the edges of deltas, estuaries and swampy land, etc.
(2) In India, the coastal types of vegetation is mainly found along the western and the eastern coastal areas.
(3) Mangrove and sundari trees are mainly found in these forests. Therefore, these forests are also known as mangroves or sundarbans.
(4) The trees in mangroves or sundarbans have oily, durable and light wood.

notes.title: (16) Himalayan forests in India.
notes.content:
(1) The forests in Himalayan range are known as Himalayan forests. Himalayan forests are classified into three types on the basis of altitude.
(2) The areas at higher altitude in Himalayan range are often covered with snow. In summer, the snow from this region starts melting leading to growth of seasonal flowing trees.
(3) The areas at medium altitude in the Himalayan range are covered with the forests having the coniferous trees like pine, deodar, fir, etc. These trees have needle-shaped leaves. These leaves allows for snow to easily glide off the branches.
(4) The areas at low altitude in the Himalayan range, i.e. at the foothills of Himalayan range, mixed forests are found. Coniferous as well as deciduous types of forests are found in this region. Sal trees are seen in greater quantities in most of these forests.

notes.title: (17) Deciduous forests of India.
notes.content:
(1) Deciduous forests are found in India in the areas receiving rainfall between 1000 mm and 2000 mm.
(2) Deciduous type of vegetation is characterized by trees that shed their leaves during dry/summer season.
(3) Trees like Teak, Bamboo, Banyan, Peepal, etc. are found in deciduous forests in India.
(4) Most of the forest area of India is covered by deciduous forests.
Chapter 6: Population

notes.title: (18) Census in India.
notes.content:
(1) In India, Census is conducted by the institute of Census of India once every ten years.
(2) In India, the first Census survey was carried out in 1872.
(3) In India, Census is conducted at the start of the decade. (i.e. 2001, 2011)
(4) In India, the next Census survey will be carried out in 2031.

notes.title: (19) Census in Brazil.
notes.content:
(1) In Brazil, Census is conducted by the institute of Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics once every ten years.
(2) In Brazil, the first Census survey was carried out in 1872.
(3) In Brazil, Census is conducted at the end of the decade. (i.e. 2000, 2010)
(4) In Brazil, the next Census survey will be carried out in 2030.
Chapter 7: Human Settlements

notes.title: (20) Importance of “Go West” policy in Brazil.
notes.content:
(1) In Brazil, rapid urbanization has taken place in the south and southeastern parts especially in the state of Sao Paulo and many other major metropolitan industrial areas.
(2) The rate of urbanization is found to be low in western and northern parts of Brazil.
(3) To avoid concentration of human settlements only in specific regions, the government of Brazil has promoted the “Go West” policy.
(4) This policy will help in reducing the pressure of population on few (southeastern) areas. This policy will also help in reducing regional imbalance in the country.
Chapter 8: Economy and Occupations

notes.title: (21) Mining in India.
notes.content:
(1) Chhota Nagpur Plateau in India is known as a big storehouse of variety of minerals. Therefore metallurgical industries are developed on large scale in the northeastern peninsula.
(2) Coal is mined in Korba in Chhattisgarh and in eastern Maharashtra in India.
(3) Marble stones are found in Rajasthan and cuddapah stones are found in Andhra Pradesh in India.
(4) In India, mineral oil wells are found in Digboi in Assam, Mumbai High in Arabian Sea near Mumbai in Maharashtra, Kalol and Koyali in Gujarat. Reserves of mineral oil and natural gas have been found at the mouth of river Godavari.

notes.title: (22) Agro-based industries in India.
notes.content:
(1) Agro-based industries are well developed at various places in India. In India, cotton, jute and sugar industries are mainly located in the raw-material producing areas.
(2) In India, sugar industries are mainly developed in the states like Maharashtra, Uttar Pradesh, etc. Similarly jute industries are developed in the state of West Bengal.
(3) In India, the forest-based industries like paper, plywood, matches, resins and lac are developed in many states near the forest areas.
(4) In India, the coir, copra and fish canning industries are mainly located in the western coastal region especially in the state of Kerala.
Chapter 9: Tourism, Transport and Communication

notes.title: (23) Development of communication in Brazil.
notes.content:
(1) The landline phone services, mobile phone services, television and radio broadcasting, computer and internet facilities, etc. telecommunication services are well developed in Brazil.
(2) Today, nearly more than 45 per cent of Brazilian population has access to the internet facility.
(3) The central and the southern part of Brazil is densely populated. The modern telecommunication infrastructure is fairly developed in this region.
(4) The northern and western part of Brazil is sparsely populated. The communication infrastructure is comparatively limited in these regions.

notes.title: (24) The main features of tourism in India.
notes.content:
(1) Tourism is one of the important occupations in India. Cultural and historical heritages, sculptures, clean seacoasts, national parks, bird sanctuaries, etc. have boosted the growth of tourism in India.
(2) Many foreign tourists visit India for exploring Indian heritage and culture, to carry our adventurous or business activities and to receive health services, etc.
(3) Since 2010, the number of international (foreign) tourists in India is remarkably constantly growing.
(4) There are plenty of opportunities for tourism at many places in India. Nowadays, ecotourism is promoted on a large scale in India.

notes.title: (25) Importance of Standard time.
notes.content:
(1) If the country has a vast longitudinal extents, there will be a great difference between the time of its places located in the eastern and the western parts. In such countries, the importance of Standard time is the most.
(2) The Standard time of a country is its official time. It is commonly accepted at all places in a country.
(3) Standard time helps in making schedule of railways or airways.
(4) It also helps in coordinating the functioning of banks, schools, colleges, markets, hospitals located in various parts of a country.
